text: This is shaping up wonderfully, thank you. Every field should periodically summarize its major advances and open problems like this.

text: Open problems! That’s a good idea. If there’s any of those in the Primer yet, it’s purely accidental – I should add some deliberately. :pray:

text: The Primer <https://akkartik.name/post/2025-06-12-devlog|crystallized something for me>. I hope you won't mind that I linked to it. It seemed more important to give credit than to not send people to unfinished drafts.

elements.text: . I hope you won't mind that I linked to it. It seemed more important to give credit than to not send people to unfinished drafts.

text: Oh that’s fine; it’s slowly transitioning into publicly-available unfinished-draft status.

text:
Thanks <@UBKNXPBAB> and <@UCUSW7WVD> for your writeups. I discover links there that I hadn't thought of before. In particular on visual programming.

<@UBKNXPBAB> classifies it as a subspecies of Live. That's probably true for most projects in this space, but I don't see it as a necessity. The one counterexample I am aware of is <@UGWUJUZHT>' diagram compiler from <http://draw.io|draw.io> to Python.

<@UCUSW7WVD> sees visual coding as a special case of domain-specific notations. If that were the case, I'd probably be more interested in the topic. My impression is that most visual coding projects see moving away from text as their main mission, independently of domain considerations.

Something else I hadn't seen before is the link between Live and overcoming the vendor/owner divide. It's hard to imagine a live system with a vendor/owner divide, but the inverse is quite possible (and again, <@UGWUJUZHT> t2t work is an example).

text: Hmmm are you saying the primer classifies visual programming as a subset of live programming? That would seem to be contradicted by “Is visual programming _live programming_? Not automatically, no!”

elements.text: Hmmm are you saying the primer classifies visual programming as a subset of live programming? That would seem to be contradicted by “Is visual programming

text: <@UBKNXPBAB> Not a subset, but a strong relation. What I found surprising is to see any link at all between visual and live programming. I have always seen them as orthogonal aspects. Your review points out that there is at least a correlation, if you do statistics across programming systems. So now I am wondering where that correlation comes from.

Your review points out that there is at least a correlation, if you do statistics across programming systems. So now I am wondering where that correlation comes from.
